brand_safety.notes: C01, C02, C03, C08 are brand-safe — they align with Dong-A's identity as a media-education institution. C04 (school visits) and C05 (instructor pipeline) also present minimal brand risk. However, C07 (theme park popup) creates a non-trivial association between Dong-A Ilbo and entertainment venues, which could dilute the premium media brand. At 90 minutes with templates, it starts to feel like an amusement activity rather than education. C10 (edu-tour) ties Dong-A to overseas travel logistics — any safety incident abroad would be devastating. The child safety and privacy protocols across all cases are well-documented, which is reassuring. Overall, the cases that stick to Dong-A's owned venues (C01, C02, C06) and institutional channels (C03, C04, C08) are safe. The further we go from media-education into entertainment (C07) or travel (C10), the higher the brand risk.

revenue_clarity.notes: C01 has the clearest revenue model: 20 teams x 200K = 4M per session, 50%+ margin. Very believable. C03 B2B model is also clear at 5-8M per corporate engagement. C09 premium tier at 8-12M is ambitious but plausible given IT sector welfare budgets. However, C04 (schools) is honestly a money-losing proposition — the plan itself admits this, calling it a 'reference investment' with negative unit economics at school budget levels. C05 (instructor training) generates no direct revenue. C06 (subscriber lock-in) generates modest direct revenue (2.6M per session with 70/30 subscriber mix) but the 'indirect subscription defense value' projections (50-500 families x 708K annual sub) are speculative. C07 (theme park) has unclear economics — revenue share negotiations haven't started, and the projected 50K/team pricing is unvalidated. C08 (government grants) depends entirely on winning competitive procurement bids. C10 has the highest ARPU but the weakest demand validation — who exactly will pay 3-5M for an edu-tour when Chosun already has this market? Year 1 realistic combined revenue across priority cases (C01 + C03 + C02): approximately 93M from SPEC.md projections, but this assumes ramp-up that may not materialize. I'd estimate 40-60M realistically.

operational_simplicity.notes: This is my biggest concern. Filamentree is a tiny team (3 core members: Jay, Ryan, JY) proposing to run 10 different case types simultaneously, each requiring different partner relationships, different curriculum adaptations, different pricing models, and different legal structures. C01 alone requires 7 staff per session. C03 requires 10 staff for 30 teams. C07 requires permanent staffing at theme parks. C05 requires a 2-week training program to run. C08 requires government procurement expertise and audit-ready accounting. C10 requires international travel coordination. The cases that Dong-A can 'approve and forget' are C01 (Filamentree runs it at our venue) and C03 (DBR-edu just connects clients). Everything else requires active coordination across multiple Dong-A departments: C02 needs DongA Science cooperation, C04 needs DongA EasyEdu school channels, C05 needs Future Strategy Lab alumni access, C06 needs Children's DongA editorial coordination, C07 needs corporate BD with Samsung C&T (Everland), C08 needs regional bureau coordination with municipalities. That's 5+ internal departments that need to cooperate. In my 15 years at Dong-A, getting 2 departments to cooperate on anything is already a significant political achievement.

strategic_alignment.notes: This is where the proposal genuinely shines. The research vault correctly identifies our gap: we have no K-12 paid AI experience education program. Chosun has EduChosun (news museum, reporter experience hall). We have nothing. The 2025 mandatory coding education expansion and 2026 AI digital textbook rollout create genuine urgency. The proposal leverages our EXISTING assets intelligently: DongA Media Center (venue), Children's DongA (subscribers), DongA Science AiR (AI platform with Minister's award), DBR Edu (corporate network), Media Frontier (government education track record), regional bureaus (municipality connections). The 3-layer strategy (C01 proving ground → C03 B2B revenue → C04/C08 public sector credibility) is sound. Against Chosun: their EduTour is travel-focused, their education programs are lecture-based. Our AI workshop is hands-on, produces tangible output (playable games), and involves parent-child co-creation. This is a legitimate differentiator.

execution_confidence.notes: Brutally honest: I have low confidence that Filamentree can deliver 10 case types. Three people (Jay, Ryan, JY) plus part-time support (Kiwon for marketing, TJ for operations) cannot simultaneously: run monthly C01 workshops, negotiate DongA Science partnerships for C02, sell to corporate HR for C03, visit schools for C04, train instructors for C05, coordinate with Children's DongA editorial for C06, negotiate with theme parks for C07, write government procurement proposals for C08, pitch to Samsung HR for C09, and coordinate international travel for C10. The plan implicitly assumes C05 (instructor training) will solve the scaling problem, but C05 itself depends on C01 being stable enough for trainees to shadow. This creates a chicken-and-egg dependency. The timeline is aggressive: C01 pilot on 2026-03-28 (1 month from now), C02 pilot in April, C03 proposals in Q2. Filamentree has proven they can run ONE workshop successfully (9 games online as evidence). But running a multi-channel education business across 10 formats with 5+ Dong-A departments is a fundamentally different challenge. The rollout sequence in the registry (Priority 1: C01/C02/C03, Priority 2: C04-C08, Priority 3: C09/C10) is at least rational, but even the Priority 1 batch is ambitious for a team this size.

hard_questions: Filamentree has 3 core team members. You're proposing 10 case types across 5+ Dong-A departments. If we approve all 10, what's your actual capacity plan? Show me a month-by-month headcount projection for Year 1, including who does what. Because right now it looks like Jay is doing everything.

C04 (schools) is a confirmed money-loser at school budget levels. You're asking us to invest our brand and school channel access in a product that loses 650,000 won per session. What's the actual business case for Dong-A here — not the 'strategic reference value' narrative, but the numbers? How many free sessions before we see a return?

The SPEC.md projects 93M Year 1 revenue. Your case plans show C01 doing maybe 48M (monthly x 2 sessions x 12 months at full capacity), C03 doing maybe 30M (if you actually land 5 corporate clients), and everything else being either speculative or money-losing. What's the realistic combined revenue if we're honest about ramp-up delays, failed sales cycles, and government procurement timelines?

You mention 'Chosun already did this' for C03 (Shinhan Bank mentoring camp), C07 (Everland commissioned operation), and C10 (EduTour). In each case, you argue Dong-A should follow Chosun's model with AI differentiation. But Chosun built these over years. What evidence do you have that AI differentiation alone is enough to compress years of relationship-building into months?

C07 (theme park) requires 6-12 months of procurement negotiation, permanent on-site equipment, rotating staff, and revenue-sharing contracts. You're a 3-person team. Have you ever negotiated a B2B contract with Samsung C&T or Lotte? What makes you think this is achievable before 2027?

The entire portfolio depends on Dong-A approving brand usage for '동아 AI Academy.' What happens to your business if our brand committee says no, or says yes but with restrictions that prevent co-branding with theme parks, corporations, or government programs?

C05 assumes 60-70% of Media Frontier graduates will pass instructor certification. You have zero data on this. What if only 30% pass? What if the graduates prefer other employment over teaching children on weekends for 400K/session? What's your backup instructor supply plan?
dealbreakers: If Filamentree cannot demonstrate a viable capacity plan showing how 3 core members + contractors can execute even the Priority 1 cases (C01, C02, C03) simultaneously without quality degradation, this is a no-go. We cannot risk our brand on a team that is structurally overextended.

If the C01 pilot on 2026-03-28 does not achieve NPS 85%+ with at least 15 participating teams, we should not proceed with any subsequent cases. The entire portfolio's credibility rests on proving the core product works at Dong-A's venue with Dong-A's audience.

If government procurement for C08 requires Dong-A to serve as prime contractor (원도급자) and assume legal/financial liability for government audits, this needs explicit board-level approval. Media Frontier was government-funded, yes, but K-Digital is a different risk profile from municipal education procurement. We cannot assume transferability.
recommended_priority: C01 — Pilot first. This is the foundation. If this fails, nothing else matters. Execute on 2026-03-28 as planned.
C03 — Start DBR-edu sales conversations in parallel with C01. B2B corporate is the fastest path to meaningful revenue. But do NOT sign contracts until C01 pilot NPS is confirmed.
C05 — Begin instructor recruitment from Media Frontier graduates immediately after C01 pilot succeeds. This is the bottleneck that blocks all scaling.

C06 — Children's DongA subscriber workshop should be the 3rd case after C01 and C03 stabilize. It directly addresses our subscription churn crisis (59K declining). But requires editorial cooperation — start internal conversations now.
C02 — DongA Science partnership is strategically valuable but requires separate internal negotiation with a different subsidiary. Defer to Q3 2026 after C01 proves the model.

C04 — Schools make sense only after C05 produces trained instructors AND C01/C03 provide reference cases. Earliest realistic timing: 2026 Fall semester. Accept the investment loss on initial 3 schools.
C08 — Government procurement is a 2027 play. Start relationship-building with municipal contacts via regional bureaus in H2 2026, but do not expect revenue until 2027.
C09 — IT premium is just C03 with higher pricing. Do not treat as separate case. Upsell naturally from C03 corporate clients.
C07 — Theme park is a 2027-2028 play at earliest. Park procurement cycles are 12+ months. Do not allocate any team bandwidth to this in 2026.

C10 — Edu-tour is the highest-risk, highest-uncertainty case. Dong-A's edu-tour existence is unconfirmed. International travel logistics are a completely different business. Deprioritize entirely until the core business (C01/C03/C05) is stable and profitable. Earliest realistic: 2027 summer.
portfolio_feedback:
The portfolio demonstrates genuine strategic insight into Dong-A's competitive position and internal asset map. The identification of our K-12 AI education gap, the analysis of Chosun's existing programs, and the proposal to leverage DongA Science AiR as a differentiator are all well-researched and compelling. The core product — a 4-hour parent-child AI workshop where kids build playable games — is genuinely differentiated. No competitor has this. The 9 published games are real proof.

However, the portfolio suffers from a classic startup mistake: breadth over depth. Ten case types with ten different partner structures, ten different pricing models, and ten different operational requirements is not a portfolio — it is a wish list. A 3-person team proposing to simultaneously serve schools, corporations, theme parks, government agencies, and international travel is not demonstrating ambition; it is demonstrating a lack of focus. My recommendation: collapse C09 into C03 (it's the same product at a higher price point), defer C07 and C10 to 2027+, and concentrate Year 1 on proving exactly three things: (1) C01 works at Dong-A Media Center with real paying families, (2) C03 generates repeatable B2B corporate revenue through DBR-edu's existing network, and (3) C05 produces trained instructors who can deliver the same quality as the founders. If those three succeed, the other cases become natural extensions. If they fail, none of the other seven cases will save the business.

per_case_notes.C01: Strongest case in the portfolio. Clear economics, minimal partner complexity, uses existing Dong-A assets. The 50%+ margin at 20 teams is attractive. My concern is the March 28 timeline — that's 30 days from now. Is there actually a contract with DongA EasyEdu? Is the venue booked? The plan says contract deadline is March 6 and venue booking needs 4 weeks advance notice. This is extremely tight. If this pilot is delayed or fumbled, it poisons the entire relationship.
per_case_notes.C02: Strategically compelling — AiR platform + offline workshop is a unique combination no competitor has. The STEM curriculum adaptation is thoughtful. But this requires DongA Science to agree to share subscriber data and co-brand, which is a separate negotiation with a separate subsidiary. The plan assumes this can happen by March 28 for MOU signing. In my experience, cross-subsidiary negotiations at Dong-A take 2-3 months minimum. Realistic pilot: Q3 2026.
per_case_notes.C03: Second-strongest revenue case. B2B corporate at 5-8M per engagement with DBR-edu's existing client network is the most realistic path to meaningful revenue. The Chosun-Shinhan precedent helps sell internally. The Net-30/60 payment terms and 30% advance requirement are well-thought-out. Risk: DBR-edu's relationships are with C-suite education buyers, not HR/welfare teams. The cross-sell to a different department within the same company is not as easy as the brief suggests.
per_case_notes.C04: The plan is honest about the economics — this is a money-losing proposition at school budget levels. The 'reference investment' framing (3 free schools as marketing investment) is reasonable but needs to be sized: 3 schools x 650K loss = ~2M investment. The education policy timing (2026 AI digital textbook) creates genuine urgency for schools. But the naiseumang (school network) AI service blocking issue is a real operational blocker that could derail entire sessions.
per_case_notes.C05: Critical infrastructure case. Without this, the business cannot scale beyond Jay personally teaching every session. The 2-week training curriculum is well-designed. The Media Frontier graduate profile (adult AI content creators) is a clever talent pool. But the 60-70% pass rate assumption has zero empirical basis. Content creation skill does NOT equal teaching skill, as the plan itself acknowledges. The compensation (400K/session, ~1.6M/month for 4 sessions) is decent but not competitive with full-time employment these graduates are seeking.
per_case_notes.C06: Addresses a real crisis — Children's DongA subscriptions declining from 80K to 59K. The subscription lock-in mechanism (workshop as subscriber perk) is strategically sound. The 50% discount for subscribers (100K vs 200K) is a clean incentive structure. My concern: the A/B test design has statistical power issues — 56 treatment families annually is too small to detect a 16%p difference at 95% confidence. The plan acknowledges this but doesn't have a good solution. Also, regional rotation (Seoul → Busan → Daejeon → Gwangju) adds logistical complexity that this team cannot absorb in Year 1.
per_case_notes.C07: This is the case I would most likely veto. Theme parks are entertainment venues. 'Dong-A Ilbo' branding next to roller coasters is not the image our editorial board wants. The 90-minute template-based 'taster' version strips out the pedagogical depth (parent-child co-founding model, deep collaboration) that makes the product unique. It becomes a glorified coding booth. The procurement timeline (6-12 months with Samsung C&T) is unrealistic for a team that has never negotiated with a chaebol subsidiary. The brand exposure value calculation (78M in impressions) is marketing math, not business reality.
per_case_notes.C08: Government procurement is a legitimate revenue channel — Media Frontier proves Dong-A can operate government-funded education. The 70/30 split (Filamentree/Dong-A) on government funds is reasonable given Dong-A provides the brand and procurement credibility. But government procurement is slow (Q4 application → next H2 execution), competitive, and administratively burdensome. The audit requirements (5-year document retention, detailed expense accounting) create overhead that eats into margins. This is a 2027 revenue play, not 2026.
per_case_notes.C09: This is C03 with a premium price tag and IT-specific customization. I question whether it deserves to be a separate case. The 'IT parents have higher expectations' insight is valid, but the curriculum difference (IT domain theme cards, parent as 'tech advisor') is incremental, not structural. The premium pricing (800K-1.2M per team vs C03's 170-250K) needs market validation. My recommendation: fold this into C03 as a 'Premium IT Tier' rather than treating it as an independent case.
per_case_notes.C10: Highest risk, most speculative case. The plan itself admits the foundational uncertainty: 'Dong-A edu-tour existence is unconfirmed.' You're building a business plan on an assumption you haven't verified. International travel logistics (visas, insurance, overseas institutions, multi-day itineraries) are a completely different competency from running a 4-hour workshop. The 3-5M per family price point targets a narrow market that Chosun has already claimed with established tour programs. This should be a 2027-2028 consideration at earliest, and only after the domestic business is profitable and stable.
